clinical_history: 64-year-old male: what do you think his chief complaint is?
diagnosis: Dyspnea. The ECG shows evidence of right ventricular (RV) overload with 1) sinus tachycardia; 2) right axis deviation, with the classic S1Q3T3 pattern; 3) incomplete right bundle branch block (RBBB) with RSR' (or qR) in V1 and S waves in lateral chest leads. There are also non-specific ST-T changes. If you were thinking that it was due to pulmonary embolus (PE) that is a good thought and was ruled out in this case. The cause of the acute/subacute cor pulmonale/pulmonary hypertension here responsible for these ECG changes was extensive pulmonary metastasis. Similar findings may occur with extensive pneumonitis, and other conditions where acute RV dilation occurs.
